entry.title: When Reliability Is the Common Language: Insights from the APM Executive Roundtable, Brisbane
entry.content:
Brisbane: An Energy Economy Under Transformation

Brisbane at night. Picture credit: storybridgeadventureclimb.com.au 

Queensland is one of Australia’s most consequential energy and resources economies. Its generation mix is shifting fast: coal’s share fell from 61% to 59% between 2022 and 2024, while renewables climbed from 24% to 28% over the same period, driven by solar, wind, and biomass. LNG exports reached 24 million tonnes in 2024-25 (5% of global LNG production), generating $15 billion in business since 2015. The state hosts 19 of the world’s most sought-after critical minerals, and its Mining, Equipment, Technology and Services (METS) sector contributes $704.8 million in exports annually.

In this environment, the stakes of asset reliability and maintenance excellence are measured in billions of dollars, regulatory obligations, and community energy security. It was against this backdrop that SAP, Wood, and PiLog Group organised a senior Executive Roundtable on Asset Performance Management (APM) in Brisbane on 22 Apr 2026, bringing together 16 leaders from the gas, power generation, renewables, oil and gas, and mining industries.

What the Industry Told Us

Despite operating across different sectors with fundamentally different assets, participants converged on the same core challenges - the common thread of ensuring reliability and delivering maintenance efficiently.

  • Criticality assessment remains unsolved. The roundtable’s longest discussion centred on a deceptively simple question: Do you know which of your assets are truly critical? The answer across the room was "it is not as straightforward as we would like". Criticality is multidimensional. It changes over the life of an asset. It depends on usage patterns, asset age, position in the process chain, supply chain exposure, changing geopolitical winds, and inherent risk at commissioning. Organisations correlating “critical” with “expensive” or “old” are making prioritisation decisions on incomplete foundations - and the answer to the criticality question is at the heart of ensuring reliability and delivering maintenance efficiently.  It impacts WHAT you do (maintenance strategies and schedules), and HOW you prepare for it (spare parts planning and supply chains)
  • Data is the opportunity nobody has mastered. Data was the most frequently discussed topic, and the most frustrating one. Organisations have more data than they can process, but the right data rarely reaches the people and systems that need it most. Work management layers are often disconnected from OT, IoT, and ERP sources. Master data quality is poor across the board, and most migrations have been ‘lift and shift’ rather than standards-aligned, carrying forward existing problems into new systems (or abandoning the gold hidden in historical data altogether in the migration process!). One telling observation from the room: rescheduled work orders are among the strongest leading indicators of asset risk, yet this signal is routinely invisible because the data never makes it into the right system. On top of that, data collection is expensive and with too much data being collected, neither the cost equation nor decision-making benefits! To illustrate this point, it was insightful to note that all the participant organisations were using or trialling to allow data captured in the field to be seamlessly transferred into the ERP source systems.  However, the temptation in this process was to ‘overload’ the field-data capture process, adding more data and fields to the information to be captured in the field, without considering the frustration this might cause with front-line workers.  Changes, however well-intentioned, must be carefully managed.  Sometimes, less is more.
  • AI interest is outpacing AI readiness. Every participant was interested in AI-driven reliability improvements. But the honest assessment was that most organisations are not yet ready to realise that value, precisely because of the data quality gap. The sequencing matters: data governance and master data integrity must come before, or at least with AI, not after it.
  • Growth adds complexity. For energy companies adding solar, wind, and battery assets to legacy portfolios, the volume and diversity of new assets create their own management burden. Automating asset build processes, integrating reliability and ERP platforms, and managing the knowledge risk from retiring workforces are immediate operational challenges, not future ones.

What Good Looks Like

The roundtable reinforced that solving APM at scale requires three capabilities working together: deep reliability engineering knowledge, leading data quality and governance practice, and integrated digital platforms and processes. This is precisely why Wood, Pilog and SAP came together for this event.

The practical roadmap for most organisations follows a clear sequence. Establish data quality and criticality frameworks first. Then connect data flows across systems so that you can apply AI to optimise preventive and corrective maintenance, identify failure patterns, and ultimately move toward closed-loop and autonomous asset management, where agentic AI continuously refines strategy based on real asset behaviour.

Organisations that attempt to shortcut this sequence, deploying AI on top of poor master data, or incomplete or incorrect maintenance records, will not get better outcomes faster. Instead, they will see limited benefits from their initial trials and will question whether there is a business case to scale these efforts to the rest of the enterprise. A guided APM-specific Health Check was proposed as a pragmatic way to get started on an Asset Management improvement journey.  In a focused engagement, it assesses data quality against international standards, functional location hierarchy completeness, criticality framework maturity, technology readiness, and consistency of business processes. This allows a prioritised, actionable improvement roadmap to be generated, without requiring a programme-scale commitment upfront.

The Conversation Continues

APM is not a solved problem. It is an evolving capability challenge in an industry undergoing structural transformation across its energy mix, asset base, and workforce simultaneously.

The organisations that lead in reliability over the next decade are investing now in the fundamentals: clean data, robust criticality frameworks, integrated platforms and business processes, and the human capability to act on what those platforms reveal.

If these challenges sound familiar, we would welcome the opportunity to continue the conversation.

What did you think about this post? Please leave your feedback, thoughts, and suggestions in a comment below.

Also, please follow the co-authors: Andrew Miller, Amarendra Singh, @pavneetbedi  so that you do not miss any future posts.

You may also be interested in the following blogs:

  • The Hidden Crisis: Mastering Asset Data to Solve the Ageing Infrastructure
  • What’s new in SAP Asset Performance Management 2604
  • What is SAP Asset Performance Management (APM)?

entry.title: How SAP S/4HANA supports operational complexity in the Oil & Gas industry
entry.content:
Introduction

When I think of the most difficult fields to work with, the first one to come to my mind has to be, inevitably, Oil and Gas. I think everyone can agree with me when I say that Oil and Gas companies are extremely hard to operate, as they do so in environments where complexity is the standard: many different parts of the business depend on each other simultaneously. Daily operations depend on large networks of assets, field activities, supply chains, maintenance cycles, compliance requirements, and financial controls that all need to work together in real time.

From the outside, it is easy to assume that with these types of companies the biggest issue would be related to the size of the operations, since they tend to have thousands of employees, global operations and massive amounts of data. Nonetheless, the real challenge is making all departments, systems, and processes work together without difficulties. In other words, it is a problem of coordination, not of size.

Over the years, the system these companies manage starts changing little by little, which leads to different teams working differently instead of following one single and consistent process, and they also begin to work with varied versions of operational data. We are talking about an environment that, as time passes by, becomes harder and harder to manage.

This is where SAP S/4HANA becomes important, not because it eliminates complexity, but because it helps organizations operate through it with greater visibility and control.

When operational complexity starts creating friction

Oil and Gas companies are, in some aspects, the same as any other type of business. One of these similarities they share is that problems don’t suddenly appear one night and explode the following day. As the company evolves, small changes, which at the time may seem harmless, accumulate. Maybe a new software is added or a different way of working among employees is incorporated. Teams become more independent as they gain confidence and news acquisitions, with their respected technology and processes, are added. All these things that are necessary in the short-term cause work to become fragmented and an environment where even the most simple activities, like generating reports or tracking inventory, is hard to manage.

We have seen cases where different departments within the same company are technically using operational data, but they are not using it in a synchronized way. This is one of the main reasons the company becomes slower and less efficient. I will mention, as examples, the maintenance team, the procurement team, and the finance department. In these cases, everyone was looking at operations from different perspectives and with different update speeds. Although everyone had the information, they had it distributed across multiple systems and processes that did not communicate consistently with each other. As a result, operational planning became slower, manual intervention was required more often, and decision-making became slower as the data couldn’t be used efficiently.  Not to mention the fact that, in Oil and Gas companies, this is particularly critical because a minimal maintenance delay can affect production schedules and have financial and operational consequences simultaneously.

SAP S/4HANA helps reduce this fragmentation by acting as a shared operational environment for the whole company. Instead of every department using separate systems and isolated workflows, they all use the same operational framework. SAP integration allows areas that depend heavily on each other to exist inside one connected environment with information being consistently shared across the whole organization.

I came across a situation like this some years ago, when we worked with a global company that supports energy and field operations around the world. This company had a very large amount of operational purchasing activity, but many of the SAP processes behind it were way too manual compared to the size of the business. When the company contacted us, they had already went through problems during maintenance shutdowns because a lot of sites were sending urgent material requests at the same time through SAP.

To help with this, we automated part of the SAP purchasing and approval process so urgent requests could move faster and teams didn’t have to spend so much time manually checking and coordinating everything. This helped them process materials faster during maintenance periods and reduced a lot of repetitive admin work between operations and procurement teams.

And that is my point: SAP S/4HANA, at least in the Oil and Gas environment, is the easiest and safest way of connecting processes in real time. One of the biggest operational pressures in Oil and Gas environments comes from the fact that many activities cannot simply “wait until tomorrow.” Maintenance schedules, field operations, procurement cycles, and inventory availability are often directly connected to production continuity. Even the smallest delay can affect the whole system in these kinds of fields.

One thing is clear: operational efficiency is not only related to production itself. In fact, if production goes well it’s because there is something else functioning behind it, and that has to do with how quickly companies are able to react when conditions change. This is one of the reasons why SAP S/4HANA has become increasingly important in asset-intensive industries. It allows organizations to build more connected operational environments where information can move faster between teams and operational areas. In industries where downtime, delays, or lack of visibility can quickly translate into operational and financial consequences, having more standardized and integrated processes becomes less of a technological improvement and more of an operational necessity.

Conclusion

In Oil & Gas, circumstances almost never slow down enough to permit a full reset. Teams have to keep the wheels turning as production can’t stop, maintenance issues pop up, materials need to be in the right place, and decisions have to be made fast, all under constant stress. Minor flaws in how companies do things can snowball into much bigger problems if no one catches them early.

That’s where SAP S/4HANA really shows its value. Sure, it covers all the basics, but it goes further: it helps companies build smoother, more connected operations. Processes speed up, data gets more reliable, and teams don’t have to waste so much time tangled in paperwork or chasing down info. In a fast-paced world like Oil & Gas, being able to respond quickly and stay consistent isn’t just nice to have, it’s essential.

entry.title: Location Master Data Replication: SAP TM to Business Network for Logistics
entry.content:
Location master data is one of the key master data objects in Transportation Management and Freight Collaboration within Business Network for Logistics. Proper location master data management ensures accurate mapping between Business Network for Logistics and Transportation Management systems, enabling seamless transportation processes and freight collaboration

Problem Statement

  • Manual creation limitations: Creating location master data manually in Freight Collaboration is not feasible when dealing with large numbers of location records
  • Maintenance difficulties with large datasets: Manual maintenance becomes difficult and error-prone as the volume of location master records increases
  • Naming convention consistency requirements: Location master codes must be identical in both Freight Collaboration and Transportation Management to ensure proper mapping between systems

Recommended Solution

Implementing the Data Replication Framework (DRF) provides automated replication of location master data from TM to Freight Collaboration, eliminating manual creation challenges and ensuring data consistency across systems

Location is a logical or physical place in which products or resources are managed on a quantity basis.

You use this business object as the basis for transportation processes. In transportation processes, you name a source location, a destination location, and any transshipment locations necessary to complete the transportation process. For this purpose, you define locations.

Tcode - /SCMTMS/LOC3

Manage location app is used to manage location master data, which was manually copied or replicated from SAP S/4HANA or created from new using the app Location master data can be used by other apps, such as Manage Entities, in SAP Business Network Freight Collaboration.

We need to create same location master data in SAP Business Network Freight Collaboration which is exist in SAP TM means the naming convention must be same.

Hence it is very difficult to create manually location master data one by one. Hence it would be better to replicate the master data automatically in SAP Business Network Freight Collaboration.

Master Data Replication in SAP TM - Configuration : 

To set up master data replication, you must configure the replication of master data via the Data Replication Framework (DRF) in the SAP S/4HANA source system. DRF uses SOAP service - LocationBulkReplicationRequest to replicate location master data from the source system to SAP Business Network for Logistics.

Tcode – DRFIMG

Define Custom Settings for Data Replication > Define Technical Settings > Define Technical Settings for Business Systems

Create a business system using your LBN ID

Select the newly created business system and navigate to Define Bus. System, BOs in the dialog structure.

Enter 189 as the BO type and Location as the description.

Enter 189 as the BO type and Location as the description.

Create a new communication channel called Replication via Services

Tcode – DRFIMG

Define Custom Settings for Data Replication > Define Replication Models

Create a replication model.

Select the newly created replication model and choose Assign Outbound Implementation.

Select the outbound implementation 189_1 and choose Assign Target Systems for Repl. Model / Outb. Impl to assign the target system (identified by its LBN ID – as maintained in the business system) to the outbound implementation.

Optional: If you only want to replicate one default language description, select the outbound implementation and navigate to Assign Language

Choose Activate

Tcode – DRFF (Optional Step)

In the worklist, search for the replication model name you have created in step 2, and define the filter criteria for including or excluding business objects

Tcode – SOAMANAGER

Activate consumer proxy: /SCMB/CO_CPX_LOC_BULK_REPL_REQ . Create a logical port and set it as the default port.

TCODE – DRFOUT

Select the newly created replication model and select the following settings under Replication Mode:

  • To send an update of all locations, select Initialization.

  • To send the lasted changed location, select Changes.

  • If you have defined your own filter criteria, select Manual. The manual replication will either be based on the filter criteria defined in step 3, or be based on the filter that you manually maintained in the DRFOUT transaction.

entry.title: Visibility Is Already There: Stop Overengineering Your Yard in SAP EWM
entry.content:
Visibility Is Already There: Stop Overengineering Your Yard!

Manyat Thinking: Simplicity is the Ultimate Sophistication

Introduction

In many SAP EWM projects, teams often struggle with yard visibility. When a truck arrives, the first reaction is usually:  “We can’t see what’s inside the truck.” And from there, the solution becomes:
          . Create new storage types
          . Create yard bins
          .  Add additional Warehouse Tasks
          . Build extra tracking logic
But what if the visibility already exists in SAP EWM — and we are simply not using it correctly ?

The Trap of Overengineering

To solve visibility problems, projects often introduce:
                  . Logical yard bins for each truck
                   . Extra Warehouse Tasks only for visibility
                   . Additional stock movements for tracking
Initially, this feels like better control. However, over time it creates:
                    . More system load
                    . Slower warehouse execution
                    . Additional process complexity
                   .  Unnecessary maintenance effort
You start with a visibility problem and end up creating an execution problem.

The Manyat Approach: Visibility Already Exists

SAP EWM already provides a natural visibility chain:

Transportation Unit (TU) → Delivery → Material
This already tells us:
               . Which truck has arrived
               . Which deliveries belong to the truck
               . Which materials and quantities are expected
So the real question becomes:
If the information already exists, why are we recreating it through additional stock movements?

Using Existing Data for Smart Yard Planning

SAP EWM is already integrated with SAP TM and delivery processes. When a truck arrives:
        The Transportation Unit is linked to a Delivery
        The Delivery already contains material and quantity information
        Expected stock is already visible in the system

Using standard monitoring tools such as /SCWM/MON, teams can:
          . Track deliveries linked to a TU
         .  Identify expected material and quantities
         .  Match incoming stock with warehouse requirements
          . Prepare labor and resources in advance
        . Plan door assignments earlier
Planning can also be improved using:
          . Delivery priority
         . ETA and waiting time
         . Queue and sequencing logic
There is no need to create artificial stock just to “see” visibility.

A Shift in Thinking

Traditional thinking often looks like this:
Truck → Staging → Planning → Execution
But a smarter approach is:
Truck → Yard Planning → Staging → Execution
This small shift changes a lot. It allows warehouses to:
          . Reduce unnecessary warehouse movements
          . Improve planning accuracy
          . Reduce system load
         .  Execute operations faster
Most importantly, planning happens before execution bottlenecks appear.

Key Insight

For planning activities, expected data is often enough.
Actual confirmation should happen during execution — not during planning.
Creating additional warehouse movements only for visibility is not optimization.
It is overengineering.

Conclusion

A smart warehouse design is not about adding more configuration.
It is about understanding what already exists in SAP EWM and using it correctly.
The visibility was always there.
We just needed to look at it differently.

By following this visibility approach, warehouse teams can reduce confusion, avoid unnecessary movements, improve truck planning, and achieve better coordination between yard and warehouse operations.

Instead of recreating visibility again and again, the focus should be on using the existing visibility effectively for faster and more controlled execution.

How do you currently manage yard visibility and truck planning in your warehouse operations? I would love to hear different real-world approaches and experiences from the community.


— Manyat Thinking
Part of Manyat Project – SAP EWM Simplified By Rahul Kumar

entry.title: New E-learning: Implementing SAP S/4HANA for Advanced Available-to-Promise
entry.content:
Is keeping delivery promises a challenge in your supply chain?

SAP S/4HANA for advanced available-to-promise (ATP), also known as Advanced Available-to-Promise (aATP), is designed to address exactly that. It evaluates supply and demand in real time to ensure accurate and dependable order confirmation dates, while protecting your business priorities and profitability goals. Built natively into the SAP S/4HANA core, it works seamlessly across all deployment options: cloud or on-premise.

Introducing the New E-learning 

Unlock the potential of SAP S/4HANA for advanced ATP with our e-learning:

Implementing SAP S/4HANA for Advanced Available-to-Promise

Through this course, you will:

  • Get comprehensive guidance on implementing SAP S/4HANA for advanced ATP to optimize order promising processes.
  • Explore core aATP capabilities, configure supply protection and product availability checks, and apply best practices for delivering reliable customer commitments in real-time while balancing supply chain constraints and business priorities.
  • Configure and implement SAP S/4HANA for advanced ATP capabilities to deliver accurate order commitments while managing supply chain constraints and business priorities.

Ready to get started? Dive into the e-learning experience now!

Note: Access to this e-learning requires a one-time registration to SAP Learning.

 

Enhance Your SAP Experience with Value Maps

Beyond e-learnings, SAP Enterprise Support offers additional resources to help you drive innovation. Value maps on SAP Community bring together guidance, recommended actions, and supporting resources to help you decide what to focus on next - all included in your SAP cloud subscription.

👉  Watch our short demo video, then head over to our page to explore our range of topics and request a call with a topic expert for guidance tailored to your goals.

 

About the author: 
Mabelle Roque Miguel is a Topic Expert for SAP Enterprise Support value maps, specializing in SAP Supply Chain Management solutions. Based in Singapore, Mabelle has been with SAP since 2007, taking on diverse roles that have largely centered around customer engagement and success. Now, her focus is on guiding businesses through their supply chain journey, helping them accelerate the adoption of SAP supply chain management solutions with value map content and SAP Enterprise Support offerings.

  Have questions or feedback around this content? Feel free to leave a comment below or contact @MabelleRM.

entry.title: 3-Level Advanced Intercompany Stock Transfer - Technical Deep DIve
entry.content:
READ PART 1 FIRST — FUNCTIONAL OVERVIEW

This document is the technical continuation of the functional blog published on SAP Community: "3-Level Advanced Intercompany Stock Transfer" (https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/3-level-advanced-intercompany-stock-transfer/ba-p/14150304). If you haven't read that one yet, it's worth doing — it covers the business case, the process flow, and the Financial Chain concept that everything here is built on.

This is the first of two technical posts. It walks through the architecture and custom development that drives the order creation part of the 3-Level STO process — the VCM configuration, the Financial Chain plumbing, the value chain steps, and the ABAP classes we built to handle them. 

 

Technical Overview

SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS2 Private Cloud Edition ships Advanced ICo Stock Transfer as a two-company process — one delivering plant, one receiving plant. But, for 3 level stock transfer we need Receiving Company that raises the Stock Transport Order, a Delivering Company that physically ships the goods, and an Intermediate Company sitting in the middle that handles the legal and financial leg between the two. So to achieve the same we had to go with custom development.

The Three Legal Entities

Each company owns a distinct set of documents in the value chain:

ENTITY

DOCUMENTS IN VALUE CHAIN

Receiving Company (RC)

Stock Transport Order (ZRC_STO), IC Purchase Order (ZRC_PO), Inbound Delivery, GR to storage location, ICo Supplier Invoice (MIRO)

Intermediate Company (IC)

ICo Sales Order (ZIC_SO), ICo Purchase Order (ZIC_PO), Goods Receipt in Transit Plant, Goods Transfer to SIT, Goods Issue from SIT, ICo Customer Invoice, ICo Supplier Invoice

Delivering Company (DC)

ICo Sales Order (ZDC_SO), Outbound Delivery (ZDC_OD), Goods Transfer to SIT (ZDC_GT), Goods Issue from SIT (ZDC_GI), ICo Customer Invoice

Intermediate Company never physically handles the goods. It exists purely in the financial and legal layer — it buys from the Delivering Company on paper, sells to the Receiving Company on paper, and the goods go straight from one physical plant to the other. That's the whole point of having a Financial Chain rather than trying to model this as two consecutive 2-Level STOs.

 

Technically we will follow the below steps in sequential manner for developing 3 level stock transfer process:

VCM Configuration Objects

The first thing we did was carve out a separate configuration namespace for the 3-Level STO. A dedicated chain category (ZIT3) and chain type (ZIC_STOCKTRANSFER_3L) mean the two processes are cleanly independent from the VCM perspective.

OBJECT TYPE

TCODE / PATH

VALUE

DESCRIPTION

VCM Chain Category

VCM_CHAIN_CAT

ZIT3

3-Level Advanced Intercompany Stock Transfer — the top-level grouping for our custom process

VCM Chain Type

VCM_CHAIN_TYPE

ZIC_STOCKTRANSFER_3L

Full value chain definition holding all 18 steps and their handler class assignments

Purchase Doc. Type

MM Customizing

ZBIC

References standard UB; assigned to category ZIT3. This is what the user creates in ME21N.

Sales Order Type

VCM Assignment

ZAIS

Assigned to ZIT3 with Process Relevance set to Delivering / Intermediate Company

Item Category

VCM Assignment

CBIC

Used for Sales and Stock Transfer items under ZIT3

Step Handler ZRC_STO

VCM_VC_CHAIN_TYPE

ZCL_PO_IC_VCMS_INIT_STO

Our custom handler registered for the initiating STO step

Step Handler ZRC_PO

VCM_VC_CHAIN_TYPE

ZCL_PO_IC_VCMS_STO2PO

Our custom handler for the IC PO creation step in Receiving Company

Step Handlers — GMs

VCM_VC_CHAIN_TYPE

ZCL_MMIM_STEP_HANDLER_3L

Shared goods movement handler registered for ZDC_GI, ZIC_GR, and ZIC_GT steps

 

Financial Chain

If you read the functional blog, you already know what a Financial Chain is conceptually. Here's what it looks like from a code perspective.

Every STO item that qualifies for 3-Level processing gets a Financial Chain ID assigned to it — stored in the custom field ZZFIN_CHAIN_ID. That ID tells you exactly which legal entities are involved, what the transit plant is, and what the delivering plant is. From that point on, every downstream document copies the ID from its predecessor. If it's missing anywhere in the chain, something went wrong.

Financial Chain Path

ENTITY

ROLE

PLANT

Delivering Company

Ships goods physically

Delivering Plant

Intermediate Company

Legal / paper entity only — no physical goods

Transit Plant

Receiving Company

Final stock destination

Receiving Plant

 

BAdI: Financial Chain Determination for STO Items

The actual determination of which Financial Chain applies to a given STO item happens via a BAdI in our case.

ZZFIN_CHAIN_ID Propagation Map

Once the Financial Chain ID is stamped on the STO item, it has to travel cleanly through every document that follows. We appended ZZFIN_CHAIN_ID to each relevant structure and set up implicit enhancements to copy it forward at each step transition. The rule is simple: wherever VCM creates a new document from a predecessor, the ID comes with it.

DOCUMENT

STRUCTURE / TABLE

MECHANISM

Stock Transport Order

EKPODATA, MEPOITEM_DATA

Structure append; BAdI populates the field when the STO is saved

ICo Sales Order Items

VBAP (custom append)

Implicit enhancement ZCL_IM_SD_ADD_FCID_IN_SO at end of IF_SD_SLS_IC_SOTOPO~SO_DATA_TO_PO_DATA

ICo Purchase Order Items

MMPUR_S_PO_IC_STO_CHG_ITEM — append ZAMMPUR_S_PO_IC_STO_CHG_ITEM

Implicit enhancement ZCL_IM_MM_ADD_FCID_IN_PO in GENERATE_NEW_PO_ITEM_DATA; reads FC ID from VBAP via the predecessor SO item

Outbound Delivery Items

OD item structure

Copied from STO item by the VCM step handler during OD creation

Material Documents

Material document item

Each goods movement step handler copies from its predecessor step

Supplier Invoice (MIRO)

EK08RZ append ZAEK08RZ; RSEG append ZARSEG

B2B interface enhancement; copied from the corresponding ICo PO item

 

Value Chain Step Configuration

The chain type ZIC_STOCKTRANSFER_3L is where the full process gets wired up. TCode – VCM_CHAIN_TYPE

STEP

DESCRIPTION

TYPE

PREDECESSOR

ZRC_STO

Receiving Company — Stock Transport Order (Initiating)

Initiating

—

ZRC_PO

Receiving Company — IC Purchase Order (to IC)

Synchronous

ZRC_STO

ZIC_SO

Intermediate Company — IC Sales Order (from RC)

Synchronous

ZRC_PO

ZIC_PO

Intermediate Company — IC Purchase Order (to DC)

Synchronous

ZIC_SO

ZDC_SO

Delivering Company — IC Sales Order (from IC)

Synchronous

ZIC_PO

ZDC_OD

Delivering Company — Outbound Delivery

External

ZRC_STO (precond: ZDC_SO, ZIC_PO, ZIC_SO, ZRC_PO)

ZDC_GT

Delivering Company — Goods Transfer (Stor. Loc → SIT)

External

ZDC_OD

ZDC_GI

Delivering Company — Goods Issue from SIT

Synchronous

ZDC_GT

ZIC_GR

Intermediate Company — Goods Receipt in Transit Plant

Synchronous

ZDC_GI

ZRC_ID

Receiving Company — Inbound Delivery

Synchronous

ZDC_OD

ZIC_GT

Intermediate Company — Goods Transfer (Transit → SIT)

Synchronous

ZIC_GR

ZIC_GI

Intermediate Company — Goods Issue from SIT

Synchronous

ZIC_GT

ZRC_GR

Receiving Company — Goods Receipt in Transit Plant

Synchronous

ZIC_GI

ZRC_GT

Receiving Company — Goods Transfer (SIT → Storage Loc.)

External

ZRC_ID

ZDC_CI

Delivering Company — ICo Customer Invoice

External

ZDC_OD

ZIC_CI

Intermediate Company — ICo Customer Invoice

External

ZDC_OD

ZIC_SI

Intermediate Company — ICo Supplier Invoice

External (B2B)

ZIC_GR

ZRC_SI

Receiving Company — ICo Supplier Invoice

External (B2B)

ZRC_GR

 

VCM Step Handler Classes

The classes below were created new or enhanced for the individual VCM steps mentioned. For the remaining steps of the value chain, we used the standard classes of 2 level STO value chain steps.

ZCL_PO_IC_VCMS_INIT_STO

Step Handler — ZRC_STO (Initiating Step)

Handles the initiating STO step. Its main job is filtering: before VCM processes items downstream, this class checks that each STO item actually has confirmed quantities. Items without confirmed qty that are also new to the IC PO in the receiving company get filtered out — they're not ready yet.

Key Methods

▸  IF_VCM_STEP_HANDLER~FILTER_OUTBOUND_ITEMS — main filter logic

▸  READ_PURCHASE_ORDER — reads STO data via CL_MM_PUR_PO_IC_FACTORY

▸  HAS_CONFIRMED_QUANTITY — checks for at least one schedule line with confirmed qty

▸  IS_ITEM_NEW — checks whether the item already has a corresponding IC PO item

▸  INITIALIZE_BUFFERS — clears instance buffers at the start of each run

 

 

ZCL_PO_IC_VCMS_STO2PO

Step Handler — ZRC_PO (IC Purchase Order in Receiving Company)

Orchestrates the creation of the ICo Purchase Order in the Receiving Company. It groups the inbound bundle items, delegates to the synchronizer class, and maps the results back to VCM as an outbound bundle. Think of it as the coordinator — the actual PO logic lives in the synchronizer and POM classes below.

Key Methods

▸  IF_VCM_STEP_HANDLER~EXECUTE — called once per inbound bundle group; kicks off PO creation

▸  IF_VCM_STEP_HANDLER~GROUP_INBOUND_ITEMS — groups STO items into bundle groups before EXECUTE

▸  IF_VCM_STEP_HANDLER~GET_EXECUTABLE_ITEMS — propagates completed items to the next step

▸  GET_ITEMS_FOR_IC_PO — extracts STO item keys from the inbound bundle

 

 

ZCL_PO_IC_SYNC_STO2PO

Singleton Synchronizer

This is the singleton that actually manages the grouping of STO items into PO objects and drives the create/update logic. It keeps a buffer (G_TAB_POM_ORDERS) of PO objects across items so that multiple STO items that belong to the same IC PO get handled in one go.

Key Methods

▸  GET_INSTANCE (static) — returns the singleton instance for the current LUW

▸  GROUP_INBOUND_ITEMS — creates ZCL_PO_IC_POM_ITEM objects and groups them into PO objects

▸  CREATE_PO_FROM_STO — fetches the PO object for a given bundle group and calls SYNCHRONIZE

▸  ADD_ITEM_TO_PO — adds an item to an existing or newly created ZCL_PO_IC_POM_ORDER object

▸  GET_EXISTING_IC_PO_FOR_STO — looks up existing IC POs for update scenarios

▸  PRELOAD_POM_ORDERS — pre-loads existing IC PO objects into the buffer for change handling

 

 

ZCL_PO_IC_POM_ORDER

PO Object Model — Order

Implements IF_MM_PUR_PO_IC_POM_ORDER. Represents a single ICo Purchase Order being built or updated. On create, it calls CREATE_PURCHASE_ORDER from the standard DAO CL_MM_PUR_PO_IC_DAO_BUILD_POH. On update, it uses GET_DATA_FOR_CHANGE and UPDATE_PURCHASE_ORDER from the same DAO. Supplier and goods supplier are determined by calling ZCL_ICSTO_HELPER.

Key Methods

▸  IF_MM_PUR_PO_IC_POM_ORDER~SYNCHRONIZE — entry point; branches to create or update path

▸  IF_MM_PUR_PO_IC_POM_ORDER~SET_VCM_CHAIN_CATEGORY — sets ZIT3 on the PO object

▸  GENERATE_NEW_IC_PO_DATA — builds PO header and calls GENERATE_NEW_IC_PO_ITEM_DATA per item

▸  MERGE_NEW_AND_OLD_IC_PO_DATA — applies latest STO changes to an existing IC PO's builder data

▸  GET_PO_ITEM_NUMBER_INTERVAL — returns item number interval based on purchasing document type

 

 

ZCL_PO_IC_POM_ITEM

PO Object Model — Item

Implements IF_MM_PUR_PO_IC_POM_ITEM. One object per IC PO item being created. It reads the STO item data (via CL_MM_PUR_PO_IC_FACTORY), picks up the Financial Chain ID from the STO item, and builds the IC PO item payload. The split criteria it calculates — STO doc number + FC ID + Sales Org + Division + Distribution Channel — determines which items end up in the same IC PO.

Key Methods

▸  SET_STO_REFERENCE — sets target operation and calls READ_STO_ITEM_DATA

▸  READ_STO_ITEM_DATA — fetches STO data via factory class; maps ZZFIN_CHAIN_ID

▸  DETERMINE_SPLIT_CRITERIA — calculates the grouping key for IC PO item assignment

▸  IF_MM_PUR_PO_IC_POM_ITEM~GET_ITEM_DATA_FOR_UPDATE — applies latest STO changes to existing IC PO item

▸  DETERMINE_PO_ITEM — finds the existing IC PO item number for an STO item in update scenarios

▸  MAP_ITEM_DATA_TO_PORDER_ITEM — maps STO item fields to IC PO builder structure

▸  MAP_SCHEDULE_TO_PORDER_ITEM — maps STO schedule line data to IC PO schedule lines

 

 

ZCL_ICSTO_HELPER

Helper Class

Central utility class for 3-Level STO operations. Determines supplier and goods supplier from the Financial Chain ID — this is where the lookup into the financial chain definition happens to find the correct business partner numbers. Also provides the CHECK_IS_VCM_CATEGORY_ZIT3 method that all the standard enhancements call to decide whether to apply 3-Level logic.

Key Methods

▸  DETERMINE_SUPPLIER — derives supplier for IC PO from the Financial Chain definition

▸  DETERMINE_GOODS_SUPPLIER — derives goods supplier (always the physical delivering plant) from FC

▸  CHECK_IS_VCM_CATEGORY_ZIT3 — returns true if the PO item's VCM chain category is ZIT3

▸  READ_BILLDOC — fetches customer invoice data and checks 3-Level sales / STO relevance

▸  ENHANCE_GR_INFORMATION — enriches invoice item data with GR details for B2B invoice linking

▸  PERFORM_3L_IC_CHECK_BEFORE_CRT — validates invoice data before creation in the B2B flow

▸  CHECK_CREDIT_MEMO_RELEVANCE — checks credit memo applicability for reversal documents

▸  PROCESS_INVC_AFTER_APPL — handles VCM integration after the invoice application interface

 

 

ZCL_MMIM_STEP_HANDLER_3L

Material Document Step Handler

Common handler for all the goods movement steps. Rather than writing a separate handler class for each goods movement step, we used one class with dedicated PREPARE_* methods per step. This class is shared with the 3-Level Advanced ICo Sales value chain too — the logic for preparing a GR document is the same regardless of whether the initiating document was a Sales Order or a Stock Transport Order.

Key Methods

▸  PREPARE_IC_GI — prepares Goods Issue from Delivering Company SIT (ZDC_GI)

▸  PREPARE_IC_GR — prepares Goods Receipt in Intermediate Company Transit Plant (ZIC_GR)

▸  PREPARE_IC_GT — prepares Goods Transfer in Intermediate Company Transit Plant to SIT (ZIC_GT)

▸  PREPARE_RC_GR — prepares Goods Receipt in Receiving Company Transit Plant (ZRC_GR)

 

 

SAP Standard Enhancements

Class Enhancements on Standard VCM Classes

STANDARD CLASS

ENHANCEMENT

WHAT IT DOES

CL_MM_PUR_PO_IC_VCMI_STO

Class enh. ZCL_PO_IC_VCMI_STO
New methods: CREATE_VCM_PROCESS_3L, ADD_NEW_ITEM_TO_VCM_CREATE_3L

Groups STO items by FC ID and creates a separate VCM instance per Financial Chain. This is what makes one STO → multiple VCM chains possible.

CL_MM_PUR_PO_IC_VCMI_OUT

Class enh. ZCL_PO_IC_VCMI_OUT
New static buffers: G_TAB_3L_VCM_PROCESS_DATA, G_TAB_3L_VCM_UPDATE_DATA, G_TAB_3L_VCM_DELETE_DATA

Holds 3-Level-specific VCM process data across create/update/delete operations. Buffers are cleared via a pre-exit on INITIALIZE.

CL_MM_PUR_PO_IC_VCMI_ADAPTER

Spots ZSP_START_VALUE_CHAIN, ZSP_CREATE_VALUE_CHAIN
Enh. on START_VALUE_CHAIN, CHANGE_VALUE_CHAIN

Fetches VCM instance GUID after chain creation and persists it to Z_VCMCHAIN_INS. Also supplies the correct GUID for delta items during change operations.

CL_MM_PUR_PO_IC_VCMI_STO

Methods: TRIGGER_VCM_PROCESS_FROM_BADI, GET_ITEMS_FOR_VCM_UPDATE, ADD_UPDATE_ITEM_IF_RELEVANT, GET_ITEMS_FOR_VCM_DELETE

Extended to handle create/update/delete flows for 3-Level; items are tagged with the appropriate operation (CREATE / UPDATE / DELETE) for downstream VCM processing.

CL_LE_SHP_ICO_VCM_STEP_IDRC

Impl. ZCL_LE_SHP_ICO_VCM_STEP_IDRC, ZCL_LE_SHP_RETRY_VCM_CHAINS

Maps VCM data for the inbound delivery creation step; handles retry of related VCM chains when the inbound delivery is processed for 3-Level STO.

CL_MM_PUR_PO_IC_VCMI_FACTORY

Enh. ZCL_MM_PUR_PO_IC_VCMI_FACTOR on CREATE_VCMI_FILTER

Sets the correct delivery class instance for the 3-Level STO scenario.

CL_SDBIL_VCM_HANDLER

Enh. ZSP_3L_SDBIL_VCM_HANDLER on IF_SDBIL_VCM_HANDLER~CHECK_CANCELLATION

Ensures the step type is determined correctly during intercompany invoice cancellation.

CL_MMIV_IC_BADI_VCM_APP_CANCEL

Enh. ZSP_IS_CANCELLABLE on IS_CANCELLABLE

Returns true when the step type is ZIC_SI, ZSC_SI, or ZRC_SI — enabling cancellation of 3-Level supplier invoice steps.

 

Include and Function Module Enhancements

OBJECT

ENHANCEMENT SPOT / IMPL.

WHAT IT DOES

Include LEINRF2I

ZSP_LEINRF2I

Sets VCM chain category to ZIT3 on outbound delivery GI posting

Include LV50SF10

ZSP_3L_STO_UPDATE_VCM_CAT

Sets correct VCM step types based on chain category during delivery processing

Include LEINRF2C

ZSP_LEINRF2C

Populates Financial Chain ID during PGR confirmation search

Include LEINRF2A

ZSP_LEINRF2A

Fetches the correct document during PGR for 3-Level STO

Include MM07MFB7_BESTELLUNG_WA_PRUEFEN

ZSP_BESTELLUNG_WA_PRUFEN_03

Skips a standard error message during GI for STO when the chain category is ZIT3

Include MM07MFB7_BESTELLUNG_PRUEFEN

ZSP_BESTELLUNG_PRUEFEN

Skips error M7-E011 if the VCM chain category is ZIT3; checked via ZCL_ICSTO_HELPER~CHECK_IS_VCM_CATEGORY_ZIT3

Include MM07MFB7_BESTELLUNG_UEBERNEH02

ZSP_BESTELLUNG_UEBERNEH02

Clears the receipt indicator (kzzug) for movement type 107 when category is ZIT3 — needed for GR in the inbound delivery to post correctly

FM MEX_PSTYP_KNTTP

ZSP_MEX_PSTYP_KNTTP

Skips the account assignment error for 3-Level STO items — the account assignment category combination is different for this process and would otherwise cause a false error

FM ME_CONFIRMATION_SEARCH_GR

ZSP_ME_CONFIRMATION_SEARCH_G

Populates Financial Chain ID during GR confirmation search for supplier invoice confirmation

 The design principle we held to throughout: no modifications to core Value Chain Framework internals, all custom logic isolated so it only fires for the 3-Level STO scenario, and the implementation stays close enough to SAP's own product direction that upgrading later won't be a nightmare.

COMING UP IN PART 3

The second technical post covers the runtime side of the process: how the Goods Movement steps are executed and timed using Transfer of Control Dates, why we switched to B2B interfaces for Supplier Invoice instead of letting VCM trigger it, how the change and delete scenarios work at the code level.

entry.title: Are You Chasing "Ghost Stock"? The Reality Check Your Warehouse Needs
entry.content:
Are You Chasing "Ghost Stock"? The Reality Check Your Warehouse Needs

Manyat Thinking: Simplicity is the Ultimate Sophistication

Introduction

In my day-to-day experience managing warehouse operations and handling logistics flows, nothing frustrates an execution team more than a system showing stock that physically doesn't exist on the floor.

You check the bin… empty.
You check the system… it insists the stock is right there.

We all know this operational headache as Ghost Stock. Whenever I discuss these visibility discrepancies with warehouse managers, the instant reaction is often to blame an SAP bug or ask for a complex system enhancement. But if you look at it through the lens of "Manyat Thinking"—where we believe simplicity is always better than over-engineering—the issue is rarely a configuration failure. It is almost always a gap in the physical movement process.

The Shopkeeper Paradox: Understanding Ghost Stock

To understand why Ghost Stock occurs even in a massive automated warehouse, we don't need to look at complex system architecture first. We just need to look at a local shopkeeper.

Imagine a shopkeeper who receives a fresh shipment of goods. On Day 1, he does a physical count, verifies everything, and stores the items on his shelves. But a few days later, during a routine stock check, he notices a mismatch. The physical items on the shelf do not match what he thinks should be there. Suddenly, he is dealing with "Ghost Stock"—inventory that exists in his mind or old notes, but is physically missing from the shelf.

What does he do to solve this? He doesn't panic; he executes a manual supply chain audit:

- He checks the original invoice to see what actually entered his shop. (In SAP EWM, this is your PRDI — Product Inbound Delivery).
- He reviews his pending and confirmed orders to see what was moved or misplaced within the shop. (In SAP EWM, this is your ORDIM_C — Warehouse Task/Confirmation).
- He tracks what has already been sold and packed for the customers. (In SAP EWM, this is your PRDO — Product Outbound Delivery).

By matching these three points, the shopkeeper instantly finds his mistake. He realizes exactly which stock was pending, which was confirmed, and which had already left the shop.

The biggest realization here is this: Whether it is a small retail shopkeeper or a 500,000 sq. ft. automated distribution center running SAP EWM, the foundational logic of supply chain visibility remains exactly the same. Ghost stock isn't a system glitch; it is a breakdown in tracking the execution journey between Inbound, Internal Movement, and Outbound.

The Reconciliation Logic: Follow the Flow

Instead of just staring at system screens or waiting for custom reports, the real solution lies in understanding exactly how data flows across the execution chain. If you look at the reconciliation diagram I attached with this post, you can see how we map warehouse behavior across those same three critical checkpoints.

1. /SCWM/PRDI — Planned Expectation
An Inbound Delivery is not just a document. It represents the expected movement.
- What material is supposed to arrive
- How much quantity is expected
This is the starting point of warehouse visibility.

2. /SCWM/ORDIM_C — Execution Reality
The truth lies in confirmed Warehouse Tasks.
- Confirmed tasks represent actual execution
- Unconfirmed tasks indicate incomplete movement
For example, if 10 units were expected but only 8 were confirmed, those remaining 2 units were never fully processed in the system. This is where inconsistencies begin.

3. /SCWM/PRDO — Final Result
Outbound Delivery reflects what actually left the warehouse.
- What was picked
- What was issued
Any mismatch here exposes hidden process issues.

The Manyat Formula for Clarity
Inbound = Intent
Tasks = Execution
Outbound = Result

When these three layers remain aligned, warehouse visibility stays accurate.

Gap Analysis: Where the Ghost Is Created
Ghost Stock is usually created because of:
- Wrong postings
- Incomplete confirmations
- Process gaps
- Data inconsistencies

In most cases, the issue is not the stock itself. The issue is the break in the movement chain.

Key Insight: Practical Action Playbook for Reducing Ghost Stock

Theoretical understanding is important, but operational control is what actually reduces Ghost Stock in day-to-day warehouse execution. Below is a simple operational approach that warehouse teams can use to identify and reduce visibility gaps.

1. Daily Triad Validation
Perform a quick comparison between /SCWM/PRDI (Expected), /SCWM/ORDIM_C (Confirmed), and /SCWM/PRDO (Issued). Any mismatch between these three layers should immediately be reviewed delivery-wise. This helps identify visibility breaks before they grow into larger inventory inconsistencies.

2. Define Clear Exception Buckets
Warehouse teams should classify mismatches into clear categories such as:
- Short Confirmation (Expected quantity > Confirmed quantity)
- Over Confirmation (Confirmed quantity > Expected quantity)
- Issue Gap (Confirmed quantity ≠ Issued quantity)
Once these categories are standardized, corrective actions become much faster and more consistent.

3. Root Cause Classification
Each mismatch should be tagged with a clear operational reason:
- Incomplete Warehouse Task
- Wrong bin posting
- Queue or timing delay
- User execution error
Over time, these classifications reveal recurring operational patterns.

4. Fast Correction Process
A simple correction workflow can significantly reduce Ghost Stock duration:
- Open Warehouse Tasks → Complete or cancel appropriately
- Bin mismatch → Recount and adjust inventory
- Outbound gap → Reconcile picking and goods issue activities
Speed of correction is critical for maintaining warehouse visibility accuracy.

5. Prevention Controls
Strong process controls reduce future inconsistencies:
- RF validations for quantity, batch, and bin
- Mandatory task confirmations
- Cut-off validations before Goods Issue
- Additional checks for high-value materials
Prevention is always more effective than repeated correction.

6. Simple KPI Monitoring
Operational visibility improves when warehouses track measurable indicators such as:
- Mismatch percentage between PRDI and ORDIM_C
- Issue gap percentage between ORDIM_C and PRDO
- Average resolution time for mismatches

Ghost Stock is not a system failure. It is a visibility issue created by incomplete movement execution. Where movement stops, visibility becomes unreliable.

My Personal Take: Movement Over Screens

In managing and designing warehouse flows, I firmly believe that technology, systems, and even AI should support smarter operational decisions—not simply automate a weak, messy, or overcomplicated process.

When you synchronize your yard visibility, staging logic, and cross-docking properly, complexity drops naturally and your inventory stays clean.

In SAP EWM, stock is not just a static quantity. It is about movement, confirmation, and control.

Conclusion

Counting stock is easy. Understanding how that stock moved is what defines a real warehouse professional. Because in SAP EWM, stock is not just about quantity. It is about movement, confirmation, and control. And where movement becomes incomplete, Ghost Stock begins to appear.

I would also love to hear how different warehouse teams identify and control Ghost Stock situations in real-world operations. Let’s share some practical fixes in the comments.

— Rahul Kumar
Manyat Thinking | Part of Manyat Project – SAP EWM Simplified

entry.title: EDI in the age of agentic AI, the structural mismatch nobody is naming
entry.content:
Somewhere in most people’s houses, there is a road atlas. Beautifully printed, comprehensive, the place you were looking for always on the join between two pages or on the fold where the binding had eaten the postcode. Then the internet arrived and you printed out a route from a website, which was a digital map in name and a dead letter on the passenger seat. The thing that actually changed driving was location services. The moment the map knew where you were and where the closures were, it stopped being an artefact and became a network you could query. Then Waze went further: routing you using information contributed by every other driver on the road. A car on the hard shoulder. A speed camera. A tailback the official feed would not catch for twenty minutes. The map was no longer a thing you read. It was a network where every participant made the whole more intelligent for everyone else.

So, time to drag this back to my actual day job, which is business networks and S/4HANA. Every S/4 programme has to answer a deceptively simple question: how will this enterprise communicate with the outside world once the transformation is done? The old plumbing breaks. IDocs are restructured or retired, partner mappings need re-testing, and on-premises middleware that has quietly carried the business for fifteen years, invisible until the moment it failed, cannot keep up. The bill comes due. But the real choice is not how to fix the plumbing. It is whether the connectivity layer should remain a way of moving documents between two known parties, or become a queryable network that an autonomous agent can reason over. The answer the market is offering, namely modernise the EDI, move it to the cloud, add a bit of AI, is the corporate equivalent of printing out a route from the website. Technically more modern. Functionally the same piece of paper.

Give cloud EDI its due first. Moving an on-premises EDI estate into a managed cloud genuinely helps: better resilience, faster partner onboarding, centralised monitoring, and no more single-point-of-failure when your one EDI specialist retires. I am not arguing against any of it. I am arguing that none of it changes what the architecture is.

Cloud EDI is still EDI. It is still bilateral. It is still static. It produces a dead document, accurate at the moment of transmission, useless the moment anything changes, and it has nothing useful to offer an autonomous agent. Agents do not just process documents after the fact. They need to interrogate live, networked data, who has capacity now, at what price, under what constraints, and EDI has no way of exposing that.

This is the part that needs spelling out. Consider a worked example. A buyer agent at a Tier 1 manufacturer receives an order for three thousand units of a sub-assembly. To place that order well, the agent needs to know which suppliers across the network have capacity, at what price, with what lead time, tariff exposure, and carbon intensity. It needs to consider whether splitting the order across two suppliers reduces risk enough to justify the higher unit price, and whether either supplier is constrained by a Tier 2 component that has gone short this week. I have sat through several of those demos this year. Not one of them could have been accomplished over a one-to-one document pipe. Not one. The agent needs a network where it can ask a question and get an answer that reflects the current state of that network.

The architectural requirement is the same whatever you call it: networked, queryable, shared state across thousands of partners, with event-level visibility and the means to act. SAP Business Network is the specific instantiation of this within the S/4HANA ecosystem. The label matters less than the property. EDI was engineered in the 1970s to do the opposite of all of it.

Watch the market on this one. Read any vendor argument for "modernising your EDI to the cloud" carefully and you will find them describing a system that decouples mapping from the ERP, holds partner profiles centrally, and offers a real-time cockpit across thousands of partners. That is not modernised EDI. That is a network with a different name on the door. The market is quietly building the right thing while loudly selling the wrong word for it.

Two numbers tell you the shape of it. Interos, in its 6 Supply Chain Risks for 2026 report, finds that 90% of organisations remain blind to sub-tier disruptions for up to 48 hours. APQC's 2026 Supply Chain Priorities and Challenges: Cross-Industry Report finds that only one in three organisations have a strategy this year to increase visibility into lower-tier suppliers. Two-thirds are not even planning to do anything about it. One-to-one connections cannot reach those lower tiers. They cannot reach an agentic workflow either.

You already know I work in this space. The companies already on a network are building compound advantage in supplier visibility, agentic readiness, and network intelligence that a one-to-one pipe cannot replicate after the fact.

This should not be a Phase 2 conversation. It should not be kicked to 2028. It should not be left for the next team to discover. By then the programme team has moved on. The decision is business as usual. Nobody around the table remembers it was once a choice. A one-to-one pipe cannot reroute when a supplier fails, recompute when a tariff lands, or answer when an agent asks it a question. A network can do all three. The companies that defer will spend the next three years falling behind quietly, and in an agentic world three years is no longer a planning horizon. It is a generation. Kicking this to 2028 is not a deferral. It is a decision to fall behind, taken by people who will not be there to see it land.

The road atlas works. So does the printed page from the route planner. Neither of them is going to tell you about the lorry on the hard shoulder at the next junction, or that a faster route has just opened up that the rest of your competitors are already taking.

entry.title: Introducing the Redesigned SAP Supply Chain Management Value Map
entry.content:
SAP Enterprise Support value maps connect you to structured, product-level groups with curated learning content, tailored recommendations, and expert guidance — included with every SAP cloud subscription. Whether you are building skills, improving operations, or planning for innovation, value maps give you the right foundation to succeed. 

One of those value maps is dedicated to Supply Chain Management (SCM), and it just got a redesign. As the supply chain portfolio continues to grow, navigating the available guidance, remote services, and enablement content can become overwhelming. That is why we redesigned it to make navigation simpler, faster, and more in line with how you actually work.

Whether you are just starting your SAP supply chain journey or looking to speed up adoption of specific solutions, the value map is built to meet you where you are, organized around the portfolio areas you already know.

 

A Portfolio-Oriented Overview Page

The biggest change is on the Overview page. As soon as you enter the SCM value map, you are welcomed with six Featured Topics, each organized around portfolio area rather than an individual solution within SAP's supply chain offering.  This allows you to quickly find the foundational guidance, services, and enablement content that matters most to you.

The six Featured Topics are: Supply chain planning, Supply chain logistics, Manufacturing, Product lifecycle management, Enterprise asset management, and Business Network, each giving you a direct entry point into the content relevant to that portfolio.

 

SAP Business Network Now in the SCM Value Map 

Another update worth highlighting is the addition of SAP Business Network, now featured alongside the rest of the supply chain portfolio in one place. 

SAP Business Network is a cloud-based network that connects buyers, suppliers, logistics providers, and asset operators. It brings together procurement, logistics, and asset processes in one place, with shared visibility and reliable data. 

To better reflect how customers run their supply chain operations, three SAP Business Network modules are now featured in the SCM value map:

  • Supply Chain
  • Logistics
  • Asset Management

 

Knowledge Base Sub-Page Refreshed

The Knowledge Base sub-page has also been refreshed. It is still your go-to starting point for foundational guidance from SAP Enterprise Support, covering design, planning, manufacturing, delivery, operations, and connected business networks. Its purpose remains to help you establish a solid foundation, safeguard solution quality during implementation and operation, and support the adoption of new innovations.

What is new is the consolidation into a single Continuous Quality Checks topic across SCM solutions, and the introduction of AI for Supply Chain.

Continuous Quality Checks for Supply Chain

The Continuous Quality Check (CQC) services are organized by SCM portfolio. CQCs are remote services offered by SAP Enterprise Support at no additional cost that analyze your project, system, or specific situations. They help identify and reduce technical and operational risks, validate solution usage and configuration in line with SAP best practices, and highlight opportunities for optimization and continuous improvement. Each CQC engagement results in a structured report that includes an executive summary, key findings, and actionable recommendations.

AI for Supply Chain

A dedicated topic covering how SAP Business AI is embedded across the supply chain portfolio. From intelligent forecasting and automated logistics execution to predictive maintenance, it is a great place to start if you want to understand and adopt AI capabilities across your supply chain solutions.

 

What This Means for You

The SCM value map redesign is more than a visual update. It is a structural alignment with the SAP supply chain portfolio, making it easier to access the foundational content for solutions under each portfolio. Whatever stage you are at in your journey, the value map now gives you a clearer starting point and a faster path to the content and services you need.

Ready to explore? Check out the Supply Chain Management value map.

 

Enhance Your SAP Experience with Value Maps

SAP Enterprise Support offers additional resources to help you drive innovation. Value maps on SAP Community bring together guidance, recommended actions, and supporting resources to help you decide what to focus on next - all included in your SAP cloud subscription.

👉  Watch our short demo video, then head over to our page to explore our range of topics and request a call with a topic expert for guidance tailored to your goals.

 

About the author: 
Mabelle Roque Miguel is a Topic Expert for SAP Enterprise Support value maps, specializing in SAP Supply Chain Management solutions. Based in Singapore, she has taken on diverse roles at SAP that have largely centered around customer engagement and success. Now, her focus is on guiding businesses through their supply chain journey, helping them accelerate the adoption of SAP supply chain management solutions with value map content and SAP Enterprise Support offerings.

 💡  Have questions, comments or feedback? Don't hesitate to comment down below or contact @MabelleRM.

entry.title: When the Supply Chain Breaks, Who Keeps the Fuel Flowing?
entry.content:
Sanctions imposed overnight. Critical infrastructure offline. Spot prices spike 15% before markets open. This is downstream operations in 2026, where competitive advantage belongs not to the largest operators, but to those with real-time decision intelligence. 

The oil and gas sector has always carried out operational complexity. What’s fundamentally different today is the simultaneity of disruption. Price volatility, geopolitical risk, regulatory intensification, and accelerated energy transition are no longer sequential challenges. They compound in parallel. 

Yet fuel must reach forecourts on schedule. Margin protection remains non-negotiable. End customers expect seamless service regardless of upstream disruption. 

This is the operational gap SAP’s Secondary Distribution Management (SDM) and Retail Fuel Network Operations (RFNO) were architected to address. 

The Challenge: Concurrent Disruption at Enterprise Scale 

Most fuel distribution operators maintain contingency plans for isolated incidents. The structural shift is toward layered, simultaneous events. A representative quarter now includes: 

Sanctions eliminate a long-standing supply relationship. Refinery outages tightening regional spot markets. EU sustainability disclosure obligations entering force. Retail margin erosion from competitors executing intraday pricing adjustments. 

Each scenario is manageable independently. Collectively, they expose every weakness in planning systems, logistics orchestration, and pricing execution. Market leaders aren’t deploying extraordinary tactics. They’re operating on SAP platforms designed for sub-24-hour response cycles instead of multi-day manual processes. 

SAP SDM: Maintaining Flow Under Constraint 

SAP Secondary Distribution Management (SDM)  handles the distribution layer by orchestrating product movement from supply points through depot networks to wholesale and commercial customers. It functions as the operational control plane for secondary logistics. 

Core Capabilities in Production Environments 

Supply corridor disruption response 
When sanctions, port closures, or infrastructure failures sever established routes, SDM doesn’t merely surface alerts. The system models alternative fulfillment scenarios in real time: which depots carry available capacity, which transport assets can be redeployed, and what cost delta results from activating secondary suppliers. These aren’t planning exercises. They’re operational decisions executed against live inventory positions and constraint-aware optimization logic. 

Continuous Product Replenishment 

Automates stock monitoring and replenishment for end-consumer sites using forecasting algorithms and ATG integration to predict delivery dates and trigger orders automatically, optimizing cycles and enhancing customer loyalty. 

Embedded compliance architecture 
Transport documentation, customs filing, and safety records generate natively within workflow orchestration. Compliance isn’t a downstream reconciliation task managed in disconnected systems. 

 

 

SAP RFNO: Margin Optimization at the Forecourt 

Where SDM ensures product reaches the right location, RFNO closes the margin loop once it arrives.

Every liter sold flows through automated settlement, with contract conditions applied, commissions calculated, and card transactions reconciled, without manual intervention or error. At network scale, that accuracy is where margin is preserved or lost.

Core Capabilities in Production Environments 

Network-wide operational visibility 
RFNO provides centralised visibility across every site: sales performance, inventory position, and stock variance, consolidated network-wide without relying on manual site-level reporting. When POS data is missing, incomplete, or outside expected parameters, the platform surfaces the exception immediately through automated error management, enabling the network team to act before settlement is impacted. 

Dynamic pricing execution 
This is where RFNO is designed to deliver measurable competitive advantage. On the roadmap, pricing strategies will integrate local competitive intelligence, commodity market movements, and margin targets, with AI-supported competitor price prediction and scenario-based pricing as part of the direction. In markets where crude benchmarks move multiple dollars intraday, the capability to adjust pump prices across hundreds of sites within hours rather than days is the differentiation RFNO is building toward.. 

Energy transition readiness 
Multi-fuel forecourts have transitioned from strategic planning to operational reality. Diesel, petrol, biofuels, HVO, EV charging, hydrogen: RFNO manages these as a unified operational framework, enabling infrastructure evolution without compromising current-period financial performance. 

Integrated Architecture: End-to-End Decision Intelligence 

The strategic value of SAP SDM and SAP RFNO isn’t delivered by either solution in isolation. It emerges from the closed-loop integration between them. 

When distribution systems maintain real-time awareness of retail network requirements, and retail operations signal demand pattern shifts back upstream, operators gain what most downstream enterprises still lack: a demand-sensing supply chain with sub-daily planning cycles. 

This translates to depot rationalization decisions grounded in actual retail consumption data, not spreadsheet projections. It enables promotional planning that factors in supply constraints. And it delivers faster, more informed responses when the next disruption materializes, because it will. 

Operational Scenarios: Real-World Execution 

Scenario 1: Supply route elimination 
Regional conflict triggers sanctions closing a primary import corridor. SDM roadmap response: within hours, volume allocation reassigns to alternate depot infrastructure, and fulfillment execution routes. Service levels hold. Customer operations remain unaffected. 

Scenario 2:  
Service Station Network Management

A network restructuring programme moves twelve stations from CoDo to CoCo over a six-month period. Each transition changes the settlement logic, the financial flows, and the dealer relationship simultaneously. In RFNO, a station's operating model and settlement behaviour are governed by master data, when status changes, PAT (Data Collation) automatically applies the correct settlement processes for that model from the next settlement cycle. No parallel systems. No manual process switching. No settlement errors during the transition window. The network changes. Operations continue without interruption. 
 

Scenario 3: Network rationalization 
Combined analytics from SDM and RFNO identify that three depot locations can consolidate without elevating stock-out risk. The fixed cost base reduces while service level agreements are maintained. 

Strategic Imperatives for 2026 and Beyond 

The oil, gas and energy sector will not simplify. Energy transition acceleration, regulatory expansion, and geopolitical fragmentation are structural market conditions, not cyclical phenomena. The relevant question isn’t whether disruptions will occur but whether your enterprise systems can absorb them without operational degradation. 

SDM and RFNO don’t eliminate market uncertainty. They provide operators with visibility, response velocity, and decision support to transform disruption from existential threat into manageable operational variable. 

In today’s market environment, that distinction separates leaders from laggards. 

About Implico:

Implico is an SAP-Branded software and consulting partner specialising in Oil, gas and BULK logistics automation within SAP-led supply chain environments. Implico provides SAP-certified solutions designed expressly to extend SAP Cloud ERP and SAP supply chain processes with industry-specific automation, delivered in line with SAP’s clean core principles. 

Implico works closely with SAP, system integrators and end customers to enhance SAP-led processes, minimise manual effort and support scalable, future-ready supply chain workflows.

entry.title: Manyat Layout: Shifting Inventory Readiness Ahead of Wave Execution in SAP EWM
entry.content:
Manyat Layout: Shifting Inventory Readiness Ahead of Wave Execution in SAP EWM

 

Introduction

While studying SAP EWM Wave Management, I kept asking myself a simple operational question:

Why do we wait for Wave execution before preparing inventory?

In many warehouse environments, outbound demand is already visible before execution begins. Sales orders may already exist, shipping schedules may already be known, and expected inventory requirements may already be identified.

This raises an interesting question:

If tomorrow's outbound demand is already known today, can part of the inventory preparation begin before Wave execution starts?

This question became the foundation of what I call the Manyat Layout Concept.

The objective of this article is not to replace SAP EWM functionality. Instead, it explores whether physical inventory preparation can begin earlier when outbound demand is already known.

Standard SAP EWM Wave Process

In SAP EWM, Wave Management is configured through standard Wave Management functionality.

Common transactions and areas associated with Wave processing include:

  • /SCWM/WAVE

  • /SCWM/WAVETMP

  • Wave Templates

  • Wave Release Processing

  • Warehouse Task Creation

  • Warehouse Order Creation Rules (WOCR)

A traditional outbound process often follows this sequence:

Demand → Wave Creation → Warehouse Tasks → WOCR → Warehouse Orders → Picking → Goods Issue

This process is proven and widely used across warehouse operations.

Most optimization activities begin after the Wave is released.

Warehouse Tasks are generated.

WOCR organizes the work.

Warehouse Orders are created.

Resources begin execution.

The Manyat Layout Concept

Manyat Layout introduces an additional preparation stage before execution:

Possible Preparation Before Standard Wave Execution

Demand → Material Identification → Replenishment → Readiness Zone

Standard SAP EWM Flow Continues

Wave Release → Warehouse Tasks → WOCR → Warehouse Orders → Picking → Goods Issue

The philosophy behind the concept is simple:

Prepare First. Execute Later.

The objective is not to replace SAP EWM functionality.

The objective is to explore whether inventory associated with expected outbound demand can be prepared before execution activities begin.

If inventory is already positioned closer to outbound operations, the warehouse may begin execution from a more prepared state.

What Is the Readiness Zone?

The Readiness Zone is not a traditional storage area.

It is not intended to become a buffer stock location.

Instead, it represents a preparation area where inventory associated with known outbound demand can be positioned before execution begins.

Only materials linked to expected demand are moved.

The concept remains demand-driven rather than storage-driven.

The objective is operational readiness rather than inventory accumulation.

What About WOCR?

A common question is:

If WOCR already organizes work, why is this concept needed?

WOCR remains an important SAP EWM functionality.

The concept does not replace WOCR.

The concept does not remove WOCR.

Manyat Layout does not ask how to replace WOCR.

It asks whether part of the preparation effort can happen before WOCR starts working.

If inventory is already positioned closer to outbound execution:

  • Picker travel distance may decrease.

  • Search activities may be reduced.

  • Warehouse preparation may improve.

  • Execution may become faster.

WOCR can still perform grouping and optimization activities according to demand and warehouse requirements.

The goal is not to replace SAP logic.

The goal is to reduce unnecessary movement before execution begins.

Another Perspective: Execution-Stage Dependencies

Another perspective involves execution-stage dependencies.

In many warehouse environments, execution activities begin only after multiple planning and grouping steps have been completed.

If an issue occurs during these stages, warehouse execution may be delayed while corrective actions are performed.

When inventory has already been prepared and positioned in a Readiness Zone, the warehouse enters execution from a more prepared state.

The concept does not eliminate system issues.

However, it raises an interesting operational question:

Can early inventory preparation reduce the operational impact of execution-stage disruptions by improving warehouse readiness before execution begins?

This article does not claim a definitive answer.

Instead, it explores whether preparation itself can become an additional layer of operational readiness before standard SAP EWM execution processes begin.

A Real-World Example

Consider a warehouse that already knows the next morning's outbound requirements.

Under a traditional approach, inventory remains in its original storage locations until execution begins.

Under the Manyat Layout approach:

  • Required inventory is identified early.

  • Replenishment moves inventory into a Readiness Zone.

  • Inventory is prepared before execution starts.

  • Wave processing and WOCR continue as standard SAP EWM activities.

When execution begins, the warehouse starts from a prepared state rather than a waiting state.

Where AI Can Help

Artificial Intelligence introduces additional possibilities.

AI can support this concept by:

  • Analyzing historical demand patterns.

  • Identifying fast-moving materials.

  • Predicting future outbound demand.

  • Supporting replenishment decisions.

  • Recommending Readiness Zone preparation.

As forecasting becomes more accurate, warehouse preparation may become increasingly proactive rather than reactive.

The Real Question

This article does not attempt to replace SAP EWM standard functionality.

Instead, it raises a practical operational question:

If outbound demand is already known, can inventory preparation begin before Wave execution starts?

And if preparation begins earlier:

Can warehouse readiness improve before execution activities even begin?

Conclusion

Traditional warehouse optimization often focuses on execution.

Manyat Layout focuses on preparation.

The concept suggests that some of the most valuable warehouse improvements may occur before the first picking activity begins.

Fast picking creates activity.

Prepared picking creates efficiency.

And sometimes the biggest warehouse optimization happens before the first pick starts.

— Rahul Kumar

Manyat Thinking

X-Raying Warehouse Operations Through SAP EWM

entry.title: Crafting Custom SAP APM Experiences with Developer Adaptation
entry.content:
Why It Matters

Your SAP Asset Performance Management Fiori apps don't have to stay locked in their defaults. With Developer Adaptation, you can tailor the interface, add new flows, and integrate features that align with your business needs—all without touching the original source code.

This approach delivers real business value by streamlining workflows, connecting external services, and creating experiences that fit your organization's unique requirements.

 

What You Can Do

Developer Adaptation empowers you to:

  • Extend SAPUI5 controls for greater flexibility
  • Add custom UI elements like buttons, dialogs, or sections
  • Create new filters and actions with your own logic
  • Design UI flows aligned with business processes
  • Integrate external services such as SAP S/4HANA or APIs

 

How It Works

Using SAP Business Application Studio, you can:

  • Create an adaptation project as the foundation for your changes
  • Use the Adaptation Editor to make graphical UI modifications
  • Deploy a variant of your app with its own ID, referencing the original
  • Benefit from automatic updates—when the base app changes, your variant reflects those updates

 

Key highlights

  • Base app remains untouched - changes live in a variant
  • Multiple variants can be built for different scenarios
  • Variants are deployed separately, giving you control over visibility

 

Where You Can Use It

Developer Adaptation is available across SAP APM applications, including Manage Recommendations, Implement Recommendations, Manage RCM Assessments, and Manage Risk & Criticality Assessments.

 

Getting Started

  1. Create an APM service instance in your SAP BTP subaccount
  2. Set up an MTA project with an mta.yaml file
  3. Generate an adaptation project
  4. Implement UI changes
  5. Preview and validate
  6. Deploy the variant and add it as a tile in SAP Build Work Zone

 

**Tip:** Disable Asynchronous Module Loading in Site Settings to avoid validation issues.

Conclusion

Developer Adaptation transforms how you work with SAP APM apps. It's not just about customization—it's about creating experiences without the complexity of modifying core code.

See also: Developer Adaptation for SAP Asset Performance Management | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: [SAP Partners and Colleagues only] Save the date for the Product Expert Training H2 2026!
