entry.title: When was the last time that you upgraded your supply chain planning system?
entry.content:
While in private, we swiftly apply the latest app updates and from time to time even switch out our old smartphones, once they become incompatible with the latest operating system, it's curious how we sometimes forget one of the most critical upgrades – the systems that drive our businesses.

Nowadays, maintaining a supply chain with outdated technology can be as frustrating as using a navigation system from the early 2000s (remember? we used to have dedicated devices for that, not our smartphones): you might get served by the technology, but you'll hardly be wowed by it. Most likely, the maps aren’t even up to date anymore; they may lead you into dead ends - or the wrong direction!
It’s time to throw away the CDs with the map updates and move to a modern, state-of-the-art system that comes with regular, automated enhancements.

Working in Supply Chain for more than a decade and with many businesses from all the different industries, let me tell you why it's time to revisit your planning application, particularly if you’re an SAP APO (Advanced Planner & Optimizer) user. The business environment and -models have changed and still do, and so should the systems that support you.
Switching from APO to SAP Integrated Business Planning (IBP) isn't just another technical upgrade; it's a step towards the future of planning, and it will change what your business can  to do – beyond what you think of as Supply Chain Planning.

Why It's Time to Move from SAP APO to SAP IBP

If you’ve been running SAP APO for years, you know how reliable and robust the system is. It does a great job, and it got many of us through some very turbulent times. But the way we plan has changed - faster cycles, more volatility, more data, and more stakeholders asking, “Can we commit?” in hours or even minutes, not days. Planning has moved from periodic, forecast-centric cycles to continuous, data-driven decisions with real-time visibility. If your team is still holding its breath for nightly batch runs, that’s your sign. Here's why you should make that leap:

  •                Planning now needs to be much faster and more agile. You need to sense demand shifts quickly, replan in minutes, and commit orders with confidence. APO’s architecture was built for time-series forecasts and batch optimization; today’s reality demands order-level agility and instant simulation.
  •                Your execution is already in S/4HANA (or heading there). The strongest planning outcomes come from tight integration with aATP, PP/DS, TM, EWM, and finance. Keeping APO in the middle adds latency, mappings, and maintenance you no longer need.
  •                Your planners need one place to decide, simulate, and align. SAP IBP gives them an exception-driven workspace, scenario management, and “what-if” results.
  •                Data is bigger, messier, and external. Weather, promotions, POS, IoT, supplier confirmations – SAP IBP is built to ingest and act on it.

Where are the SAP APO Modules going?

The “network centric” planning parts of SAP APO, Demand Planning and Supply Network Planning, will be covered by SAP IBP, and enhanced with a lot of more capabilities that I will mention in the next part.

The plant- centric parts have moved into SAP S/4HANA for tighter execution integration.

And SAP IBP is not a 1:1 exchange for SAP APO. It comes with a long list of advantages:

  1. Real-time planning and analytics without data replication
    SAP IBP offers real-time planning and analytics, enabling faster decision-making compared to SAP APO. It offers advanced scenario management and simulation for efficient exploration of planning strategies. Additionally, it enables data aggregation and drill-down, providing detailed insights into business operations.
    Furthermore, it not only plans on units, but can convert these numbers into monetary value – allowing for decision making based on revenue and cost, not on volume. Advanced analytics help with visualization and evaluation.
  2. Collaboration and process support
    While APO was a solid planning tool, it was hard to  collaborate across the enterprise. However, to create the best possible plan and to agree upon that, a lot of stakeholders need to work together. SAP IBP comes with its own set of process and decision support, review cycles, and a collaboration platform. Process steps can be automated, and alerts allow for management by exception
  3. A true and native cloud environment
    with all the associated benefits. Regular updates and new functionality, better scalability, browser-based access, integration with other tools. Delivering more flexibility & agility to succeed in an extremely dynamic business environment.
  4. Internal and external integration
    SAP IBP is an end-to-end tool for supply chain planning, covering demand, inventory and supply planning, from strategic over tactical to operational level. Simulating an increase in demand? You can immediately see the effects on the inventory and supply side.
    Moreover, integration with other systems such as the Business Network, SAC or SAP S/4 HANA allow for processes and collaboration across tools, departments and even companies.
  5. New set of functionality
    SAP IBP offers a whole set of capabilities that SAP APO did not offer.
    Starting from true S&OP process support, over Demand Sensing, Multi Stage Inventory Optimization, extensive Scenario and Simulation capabilities…and many more. We will deeper dive into this topic on the next blog.
  6. Embedded AI
    Last but not least, of course there is extensive AI functionality built into the tool. From the conversational agent, Joule, that helps planners through a chat function, over advanced Planning Assistance, to AI-assisted optimization analysis for better understanding and evaluation of forecasting & optimization results, there is a broad range of use cases covered. Not as an add-on, but truly embedded.

In summary, SAP IBP is not just an upgrade but a mature platform leveraging the latest technologies to deliver a future proof planning platform. The world of supply chain planning has moved to the next stage, and SAP IBP combines best-of-breed with best-of suite advantages. As businesses embrace new technologies to keep pace with the market, clinging to APO is like holding on to your old navigation device. At best, it might lead you onto a very bumpy road.
It's time to let go of the old and embrace the new; update your supply chain planning tool to unleash you full business potential.

Stay tuned for the next part where we'll explore why choosing SAP IBP over other supply chain planning solutions is your best strategic move, bringing capabilities you never imagined were possible.

If in the meantime, you want to learn more, you can visit:

  • Modern Supply Chain Planning – a Strategic Guide
  • Unlock your supply chain potential with SAP Integrated Business Planning AI
    to learn about AI in SAP IBP
  • What is SAP Integrated Business Planning?
  • SAP IBP webcasts
  • A Predictable Path to IBP: Proven Approaches from the SAP Partner Ecosystem
    Whitepaper

This blog is AI infused 😉

entry.title: Why SAP for Integrated Business Planning?
entry.content:
In the last part, I was already explaining why it’s time to upgrade your Supply Chain Planning system.

In this part, I want to deeper dive into the reasons, why you should choose SAP Integrated Business Planning (IBP) over other Supply Chain Planning solutions, specifically if you are coming from SAP APO.
Before looking at the tool and its capabilities, let me give you some general benefits of choosing the SAP platform:

1. Quarterly cloud releases included with your subscription

You will leverage innovations as new capabilities are delivered automatically every quarter, at no additional cost. This means, you buy into a tool that is evolving and getting new enhancements, not a custom project that will need costly upgrades every time you need adjustments. Maintenance is also included with the subscription model.  There is even a customer influence portal where you and other customers can suggest topics for improvement.

2. A strong partner ecosystem that understands your requirements and SAP

There is a long list of certified partners around the globe with proven SAP IBP experience, industry templates, and accelerators. They bring in industry expertise and know-how, from huge transformation projects down to system configuration. You can choose from specialized niche partners to global system integrators for complex, multi-country rollouts, depending on your businesses set up and requirement.

3. Preconfigured content and best practices

Templates and best practices with proven strategies and frameworks are available to facilitate faster implementation, reduce risks, and hence improve time to value. Industry-tailored content is available for better fit to purpose without the need for excessive customization. Again, they come at no additional cost.

4. Transparency

Not a single other provider is as transparent with functionality, knowledge and plans:
You can now go and access the help documentation, watch countless web-sessions, read the (publicly available!) roadmap on what is planned in future releases. Repeating myself, but at no additional cost.
This makes it easy for projects leads to understand capabilities, as well as for users to learn and get trained, and also decreases investment risk. (Find links below)

5. A large and active community for faster adoption

SAP IBP has far more than a thousand customers. There are customer success days, customer network meetings, and manyfold possibilities to exchange with other users to learn from their experiences, use cases and pain points.
Furthermore, there are dedicated SAP IBP topic pages with Q&A, blogs, and product guidance. As a user, you’re not alone. It’s pretty sure there will be other companies from the same industry, size, region, or with the same use case that you can reach out to and connect with.

6. Cross-system Integration

SAP offers pre-built integration templates to support plan-to-execute and other critical business processes across tools. Native integration to many SAP tools as well as detailed documentation on how integration can be set up facilitate and speed up the process.
Cross-tool processes like xP&A (extended Planning and Analysis with SAP Analytics Cloud) for true financial integration, SyP (Synchronized Planning with SAP S/4HANA Manufacturing for Planning & Scheduling), MRO (Maintenance, Repair and Operations with SAP Plant Maintenance) or Supply Chain Collaboration with SAP Business Network help overcome departmental silos or enable multi-enterprise processes with partners.
Moreover, non-SAP systems and data sources can be connected, giving you the flexibility and possibility to create a wholistic picture of your supply chain.

7. Best of breed in a suite

SAP IBP combines best of breed capabilities, like its demand planning and multi echelon inventory optimization, that can compete with any system out there, with the advantages of a best of suite approach: a single platform with a common data model, embedded analytics and collaboration, and native integration with SAP S/4HANA and the broader SAP portfolio for end-to-end, real-time planning.

8. Customer praise

As of Nov 2025, SAP IBP has been recognized as a clear leader in supply chain planning among customers, and that across several major software review platforms. Don’t believe me? You can read more here.

Could I get you excited about all the benefits that the SAP IBP platform can offer?

We didn’t even get to features and functions! Grab a coffee and get ready for what the tool can offer over good old APO.
If you are looking for basic “what is SAP IBP” information, I included a like into the resource section.

1. End to End Supply Chain Planning in one tool

This was already called out, but it cannot be emphasized enough. SAP IBP both covers all the process steps for Supply Chain Planning, from Demand, over inventory, to supply, and on Strategic, tactical and operational level, in a unified suite, enabling the convergence of supply chain silos.
All of this in one tool, based on one data model, with one user interface.

Where APO needed to release demand plans to the supply plan, this can work seamlessly and instantly within one planning area (or disconnected, but only if you want to). Changes can be reflected in real time across all process steps.
This means planners don’t need to jump between planning views or even systems. 

2. Integrated decision-centric planning

Historically, S&OP was the first process covered, and it was the starting point of SAP IBP. I specifically want to call it out here because it summarizes many of the news compared to APO: a collaborative, iterative process supported by dedicated process & task management, that includes stakeholders from beyond Supply Chain, that includes value planning for better decision making. But that holds true for the whole platform, not only for S&OP process support.  

3. Scenarios & simulation

Creating scenarios was possible in APO, but cumbersome and lengthy. Planning versions needed to be copied and adjusted. It wasn’t flexible, ad hoc simulation.
In SAP IBP, any planner can create a new scenario with some clicks. Adjust it, compare it, share it with other users, and simulate changes. If agreed upon, a scenario can be “promoted”, becoming the new base version.

4. Unified supply chain leader & planner workspace

APO was a very strong planning tool. It was a tool for power users. For expert planners, that knew their transactions well. For them, it did a great job.
These days, more and more occasional or light users need to be included in planning cycles. Be it a Sales rep who enters a forecast for his region. Be it a financial planner looking at the reconciliation review.
SAP IBP has a one-stop shop workspace for a broader range of users. Role-based and app-style access, catering for users in IT & business, from Master Data Management, over Excel-based planning tables, to highly adjustable Planner Workspaces. With state of the art built in analytics, dashboards, and maps, that make the life of a planner easier.
As change management and the war for talent are ongoing challenges for businesses, SAP puts people at the center of the transformation.

5. Collaboration

SAP IBP has different collaboration support functions embedded into the system, like dedicated Process Management, enabling & tracking tasks, cell-level comments, attachments, and shared views/versions. It also drives issue-based collaboration through alerts shared dashboards—supporting notifications, what-if scenarios, and fast cross-functional alignment. Furthermore, MS Teams integration is possible.

6. New Planning capabilities

After S&OP, new functionality was brought into the platform that hadn’t been covered inside APO. At the beginning, those were the Supply Chain Control Tower, Demand Sensing, and Inventory Optimization. Over time, more and more functionality was added, by far surpassing the capabilities of APO by now.
For example, extensive forecasting and demand planning capabilities, include pre- and post- processing, a long list of statistical models, pick-the-best and ML based algorithms.
Or driver based planning, that let’s you model business drivers (like promotions, or, maybe, and imposed tariff) to see and manage the impacts of these events.
Or true multi-stage inventory optimization that simultaneously calculates inventory targets across the whole network.
Last but not least, data-driven post planning intelligence enables planners and process specialists to monitor KPIs to review performance and find possibilities for improvement.

7. Business AI 

Last, but not least, of course, we do need to mention AI.
SAP IBP comes with a long list of ML and AI capabilities that could fill (and do fill, see link section below) its own blog post. 
ML-based functionality has been part of the platform for several years already, like different forecasting algorithms or cluster-based alert detection. 
More and more use cases are being explored and added, like Joule, SAP's generative AI assistant. These use cases vary from pre-process support, such as Master Data Consistency Checks, process support like job scheduling and batch job anomaly detection, to planner support like AI-assisted optimization analysis for supply & inventory optimization. 
As the AI use cases and capabilities are moving faster than I am - please check our latest blogs & webcasts specific to the topic. 

So, if you are evaluating where to go with your Supply Chain Planning (be it based on APO or any other legacy landscape), do take a closer look at SAP IBP.

It might be the start of a great, transformative journey into the new world of planning.

 

If you want to learn more, here are some interesting resources:

  • First part of this blog post
  • SAP IBP help documentation
  • SAP IBP webcasts
  • Unlock your supply chain potential with SAP Integrated Business Planning AI
    blog post
  • SAP Integrated Business Planning Annual Report 2025 for customer stats, stories, and product highlights
  • SAP Supply Chain Planning Success Story Book
  • SAP IBP Roadmap Explorer
  • SAP IBP community
  • SAP IBP customer influence portal

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Cloud and SAP SCM Enablement: Live Product Expert Sessions in 2026
entry.content:
As organizations accelerate their journey toward the Autonomous Enterprise, SAP is redefining how business processes are executed through AI, automation, and data-driven decision-making. With SAP’s recent Autonomous Enterprise vision now rolled out, enabling users to fully leverage SAP S/4HANA Cloud and SAP Supply Chain Management (SCM) solutions has become more important than ever.

We at Cloud ERP & SCM Product Success Enablement and Adoption strive to enable our valued customers and partners to take advantage of our suite solutions. Therefore, we offer various enablement assets, from product experts to product experts, explaining the features and functions as well as the benefits of innovations across the suite.

In this blog, we showcase the diverse live session formats delivered for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, and SAP Supply Chain Management. From expert-led webinars to deep-dive learning experiences, these programs are designed to help customers and partners stay current with innovations, build practical expertise, and accelerate adoption of SAP S/4HANA Cloud and SAP SCM solutions. Explore how each format supports different learning needs and empowers participants to get the most value from their SAP investments.

 

In the following, I'd like to shed some light on our various live product expert sessions. As of today, the team offers five different types of live sessions:

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: Early Release Series | Equivalent: What’s New for SAP SCM
  • In-Depth with SAP Cloud ERP | Equivalent: Meet the Expert for SAP SCM
  • Product Expert Training for the Autonomous Enterprise

 

This table helps you to get an overview of the available sessions as well as the content that is covered during the different formats. If you're interested in more detail and would like to follow regular updates, check out the individual landing pages linked below.

What’s New for SAP SCM

Talk about and demo key innovations for major SAP SCM releases

Bi-yearly

Customers, partners, SAP Internals

ON24 webinars, open & free for anyone

Meet the Expert for SAP SCM

Deep-dive knowledge transfer sessions independently from releases for SAP SCM

Monthly based on demand

Customers, partners, SAP Internals

ON24 webinars, open & free for anyone

Product Expert Training H1

Snackable standalone expert videos covering broad and deep-dive product knowledge across the suite

Always-on in H1 starting mid-March – end of June

Open to anyone

Via learning.sap.com

Product Expert Training H2

~ 3 full weeks of training covering broad and deep-dive product knowledge across the suite

Hybrid event in H2 (starting 28th Sept. 2026)

Partners and SAP internals

Active SAP Learning Hub License needed**

*Get access to Support Edition for Customers & Partners // Internal Edition for SAP Employees.
**Access Partner Edge here or send a mail to partner@sap.com if you face any issues.

 

Live Product Expert Sessions Throughout 2026

Fig. 1: Global enablement plan for 2026; based on SAP’s release plan. As the release plan may be changed by SAP without notice, the global enablement plans can change as well.

 

With the above focus of the sessions in mind, let's take a look at the global live session plan for 2026. For the two major releases SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608, we plan on showing and demoing highlights of the releases during the Early Release Series which is happening one week before shipment.

Similar to the offering for the Public Cloud, we host a webinar series to cover the latest features in the area of SAP SCM via the What’s New format.

In an almost monthly cadence, based on demand, we're hosting In-Depth with SAP Cloud ERP which is a format that is running independently from releases. In these sessions, we cover specific topics in-depth to get a more holistic insight into certain aspects of the product. As this series is not bound to releases, it depends on topic suggestions from our stakeholders. If you'd like to nominate topics, simply do so via this blog post.

Similar to this In-Depth series, we host an event called Meet the Expert which is targeted for deep dive insights into SAP SCM solutions.

In H1 2026, we offer the Product Expert Training in an always-on format with standalone expert videos and bite-sized learning content covering the Autonomous Suite. In the second half of the year (H2) 2026, the Product Expert Training for the Autonomous Suite will take place in a hybrid mode. The event will combine an onsite week in St. Leon-Rot with multiple weeks of virtual sessions, enabling partners to engage directly with SAP product experts and executives in person, while also allowing broader participation through live online access.

 

 

How to sign up for the SAP Enterprise Support Edition as a customer/partner

In order to watch the recordings, you will need to have an active SAP Learning Hub license with at least the SAP Enterprise Support Edition. Find out how to register for the SAP Enterprise Support edition in case you don't already have access to any of the editions yet:

  • Navigate to the sign up page
  • Click the Sign up button. Authenticate yourself with your s-user ID and password. Upon first access, the system will check your eligibility, create a new SAP Learning Hub user, and populate your learning catalog respectively. Within two hours, you will then receive a registration confirmation via e-mail.
  • After you have received the confirmation e-mail, the registration process is complete.

Partners can access the recordings through an SAP Partner Edge Edition.

 

How to sign up for the Internal Edition as an SAP employee

In order to watch the recordings, you will need to have an SAP Learning Hub license. In case you have no edition assigned to your profile, please register here for the Internal Edition which is free of charge.

Make sure to be logged onto the SAP Learning Hub before opening the URL. In case the link doesn't open, copy and paste it into your browser. If you face any other issues with your login, please contact the help center.

 

Follow the PSCC_Enablement tag to stay up to date with the latest blog posts.

 

Find All of Our Assets

Is there more enablement we’re offering? Of course! We offer a podcast, different video formats, demo playlists, customer councils and much more. Check out our interactive wheel:

entry.title: Choosing Between SAP Extended Warehouse Management and SAP Logistics Management
entry.content:
SAP's warehouse management portfolio now offers three distinct paths: SAP Extended Warehouse Management for Private Cloud, SAP Extended Warehouse Management for Public Cloud, and the newer warehouse management solution released officially for customers in March 2026: SAP Logistics Management

In this blog, I would like to help customers select the best path between SAP Extended Warehouse Management for Private Cloud and SAP Logistics Management, based on their intralogistics profile.

What Is SAP Logistics Management?

Before comparing the solutions, let me address the most frequent question I hear from customers: "What is SAP Logistics Management?"

In a nutshell, SAP Logistics Management is a cloud-based logistics solution that combines warehouse management, transportation management capabilities, and Business Network for Logistics to optimize operations through network-centric collaboration. As a new SaaS offering, it unites warehousing and transportation dispatching, helping businesses optimize their logistics fulfillment operations and improve overall efficiency.

Setting the Foundation

This high-level definition serves as an initial foundation for any customer planning to implement an SAP warehouse management solution. The natural next question within the organization is: Which solution best fits my operations?

Both solutions serve distinct warehouse business models, and this statement should guide you on solution evaluation and due to this I would like to provide clarity and help customers avoid common mistakes when deciding between SAP Extended Warehouse Management for Private Cloud (Embedded or Decentralized) and SAP Logistics Management.

Core Principle

Before starting any discussion, it is essential to understand one key point:

SAP Logistics Management does not replace SAP Extended Warehouse Management for Private Cloud.

Instead, SAP Logistics Management extends SAP's warehouse portfolio, enabling organizations to optimize every site—not just large and complex distribution centers.

These two solutions serve different intralogistics profiles, with a deliberate overlap zone where either option may be appropriate depending on specific business needs.

The diagram below shows, at a high level, which solution is recommended based on the intralogistics profile. In the overlap zone (highlighted in yellow), both solutions might be applicable. Let us take a deeper look at the different features and scenarios that can guide you toward the right decision

IMPORTANT: Unlike SAP Extended Warehouse Management for Private Cloud—where new features or enhancements are released with each Feature Package Stack (FPS) or new SAP S/4HANA version—SAP Logistics Management delivers new features and enhancements on a bi-weekly basis. Visit  What’s New Viewer regularly to stay up to date with the latest content.

When to Choose SAP Logistics Management

SAP Logistics Management is best suited for organizations with low-to-mid complexity warehouses. It is designed for smaller warehousing and shipping facilities, scaling businesses, and second-tier operations of large enterprises. It seamlessly integrates with your core SAP S/4HANA Cloud (ERP) order processing and your network of logistics service providers and carriers.

Typical Characteristics

  • Full transparency into logistics operations
  • Scalable growth
  • Ease of access to carrier networks
  • Low-to-medium operational complexity
  • Need for rapid deployment across multiple sites
  • Focus on guided execution rather than deep optimization
  • Desire to eliminate paper-based processes and digitize operations
  • Limited or no advanced automation or robotics
  • Strong emphasis on speed of implementation and lower TCO

Common Warehouse Types

  • Local or regional replenishment centers
  • E-commerce micro-fulfillment centers
  • Production-support warehouses
  • Service and MRO warehouses for internal consumption

When to Choose SAP Extended Warehouse Management

SAP Extended Warehouse Management is designed for highly complex, high-volume warehouse operations requiring advanced optimization and automation.

Typical Characteristics

  • High throughput and performance requirements
  • Peak season processing with large volume spikes
  • Very high task volumes (thousands per hour)
  • Advanced wave management with multiple warehouse requests
  • Intensive use of transport units
  • Integration with warehouse automation technologies

Supported Automation

  • Conveyor systems
  • AS/RS (Automated Storage and Retrieval Systems)
  • AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)
  • AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots)
  • Material Flow System (MFS)
  • Voice-directed picking

Advanced Capabilities

  • Complex process models (POSC/LOSC)
  • Cross-docking and merchandise flow-through
  • Yard management integration
  • Slotting and rearrangement
  • Task interleaving
  • Labor management and demand planning

The Overlap Zone: When Both Could Fit

Some warehouses fall into a gray area where either SAP Logistics Management or SAP Extended Warehouse Management could be appropriate. In these cases, a deeper evaluation is required.

Choose SAP Logistics Management if:

  • You want a unified platform combining Transportation, Warehouse Management, and Business Network
  • You prefer a modular, low-complexity solution
  • You need a simple execution layer
  • Automation is limited or moderate
  • You prioritize cloud-native architecture
  • You aim for reduced total cost of ownership (TCO)
  • You require a global template with fast rollout capabilities

Choose SAP Extended Warehouse Management if:

  • Your strategy is evolving toward advanced automation
  • You operate in a complex IT landscape integrating multiple ERP systems
  • Advanced routing and picking optimization are must-have capabilities
  • Your warehouse is expected to scale into a high-throughput environment
  • You handle complex exception scenarios
  • You plan to leverage Labor Management, advanced interleaving, or AI-driven optimization

Conclusion

The purpose is not to frame the decision as "old vs. new," but rather as "best fit for each site."

Organizations with diverse warehouse networks may benefit from using both solutions:

  • SAP Extended Warehouse Management for Private Cloud for high-throughput and complex distribution centers
  • SAP Logistics Management for smaller, less complex sites that still require structured, digital execution

Ultimately, the key question is not: Which solution is better? But rather: Where is each warehouse today—and where is it going?

entry.title: Get Ahead of What's Next: Join the Early Release Series
entry.content:
Be First to Know What’s Next

Curious about the innovations shaping the future of supply chain? The Early Release Series gives you a front row seat. It's a biannual event where SAP product experts walk you through what's coming next in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, before everyone else.

What You'll Get Out of It

Across several days, you can join expert-led demos, gain insider insights, and take part in real-time Q&A. That means early access to new features and direct answers from the people building them. It's a practical way to see live demos, ask your questions, and plan ahead with confidence.

Sessions for the SCM Community

Here are four sessions we think you'll find valuable. Each one is a detailed, expert-led deep dive.

Artificial Intelligence — July 13, 2026, 12:30pm – 2:00pm CET

Take a broader look at where AI is heading. This session covers the Autonomous Enterprise, Joule for Business, workflow automation, and Autonomous SCM, including production, planning, and inventory agents that connect directly to your supply chain work.

Supply Chain Management — July 16, 2:00pm – 2:45pm CET

Discover the latest innovations in Supply Chain Management and see how they can support your day-to-day operations.

Manufacturing — July 14, 4:00pm – 4:45pm CET

Explore the newest manufacturing innovations, from engineering and planning to shop-floor execution. You'll see how AI-driven manufacturing agents help streamline decisions, automate routine tasks, and strengthen operational resilience. Expect practical demos, insider tips, and a clear view of what's next, so you can accelerate value from day one.

Quality Management — July 16, 4:30pm – 5:15pm CET

Get up to speed on the latest innovations in Quality Management and what they mean for your processes.

 

How to Sign Up

An active SAP Learning Hub license is required to register for the live sessions and to watch the recordings afterward.

If you're a customer:

You'll need access to at least the SAP Enterprise Support edition. Here's how to sign up:

  1. Go to the sign-up page and select Sign up.
  2. Authenticate with your S-user ID and password.
  3. The system checks your eligibility, creates your SAP Learning Hub user, and populates your learning catalogue. This step can take up to 2 hours.
  4. You'll get a confirmation e-mail once registration is complete.

If you're a partner:

With a valid SAP Universal ID and access to the Empowering SAP Partners site, you can join the live sessions and recordings directly.

Don't Miss Out

These sessions won't wait, and neither should you. Secure your spot to gain early insights, watch live demos, and stay ahead with the newest SAP updates.

Register today and be first to know what's next: learning.sap.com

entry.title: Power up your SAP projects in Q3 with the SAP Enterprise Support Advisory Council!
entry.content:
Looking for more hands-on guidance and early access to the latest SAP Enterprise Support services? With SAP Enterprise Support, part of the Foundational Success Plan included in all cloud subscriptions, you already have a strong baseline to get started quickly, safeguard business continuity, and continuously realize value.

Through SAP Enterprise Support Advisory Council (ESAC) , you can go even further. As a cornerstone of this offering (and with no additional cost!), ESAC connects you directly with SAP experts and peers to bring practical, hands-on support to your projects, pilot new services ahead of general availability, and shape the future of SAP support.

Why join?

  • Get expert guidance for your projects
  • Access and influence new services before general release
  • Turn your feedback into real support improvements

Your commitment is minimal (only a few hours of engagement, depending on the workstream). In return, we simply ask for your honest feedback to help us continuously improve our services.

 What’s New in Q3?

  • EGI*: Configuring SAP Enterprise Service Management for Go-Live (Blog)
  • EGI: Enabling AI Features in SAP Sales & Service Cloud Version 2 (Blog)
  • CQC* : Transformation Assessment for Sustainability (Blog)

Still open to new customers?

  • CQC Transformation Assessment for SAP Transportation Management (Blog)
  • CQC Transformation Assessment for SAP Extended Warehouse Management (Blog)

Get Involved? 

  • Learn more: visit us on SAP Support Portal. 
  • contact us:  esac@sap.com

Your SAP Enterprise Support Advisory Council team

*Continuous Quality Check (CQC): Work with our experts to analyze your system using real data and receive a report with key findings and a clear action plan to improve outcomes and reduce risks.

*Expert-Guided Implementation (EGI): A multi-day, remote, workshop-style session where participants learn to complete a defined activity with the support of experienced SAP service engineers.

entry.title: Cross Docking Is Not Speed — It Is Controlled Movement
entry.content:
Cross Docking in SAP EWM: Why Synchronization Matters More Than Speed

Tags: SAP Extended Warehouse Management, SAP Transportation Management

Cross Docking in SAP EWM Why Synchronization Matters More Than Speed

Introduction

Cross Docking is often described as a warehouse process that moves inbound goods directly to outbound delivery without long-term storage. While this definition is technically correct, it can also be misleading.

During my study of warehouse execution scenarios in SAP EWM, I began asking a different question:

Is Cross Docking really about moving goods faster, or is it about synchronizing warehouse and transportation activities?

In real warehouse operations, moving a pallet from one location to another is rarely the biggest challenge. The real challenge is ensuring that the correct material reaches the correct outbound shipment at the correct time while maintaining operational stability.

From this perspective, Cross Docking becomes less about speed and more about controlled synchronization.

The Common Misunderstanding

Cross Docking is commonly associated with three benefits:

  • Reduced storage time
  • Faster material movement
  • Lower inventory handling

These benefits are real, but they only exist when warehouse execution and transportation execution remain synchronized.

Consider the following situations:

  • The outbound vehicle arrives later than planned.
  • A staging area has reached capacity.
  • Labour resources become unavailable.
  • Transportation priorities change after inbound goods have already arrived.

In these scenarios, simply moving material faster does not improve warehouse performance. Instead, uncontrolled movement may create additional handling, staging congestion, or incorrect loading sequences.

The objective therefore shifts from fast execution to controlled execution.

A Railway Station Analogy

A busy railway station provides a useful analogy.

Passengers arrive on one train and transfer to another. At first glance, this resembles Cross Docking because people move directly from one transportation flow to another.

However, when delays occur, passengers are not simply pushed onto the next platform.

Instead:

  • Waiting areas are used.
  • Platforms are reassigned.
  • Passenger flow is redirected.
  • Station operators continuously adjust movement until connections become synchronized again.

The railway station is not optimizing walking speed.

It is optimizing coordinated movement.

Warehouse Cross Docking operates in a very similar way.

How This Translates into SAP EWM

In SAP EWM, Cross Docking is supported by execution processes that connect inbound logistics with outbound requirements.

Depending on the business scenario, SAP EWM may use:

  • Warehouse Tasks (WTs) to move goods directly toward outbound staging.
  • Storage Types and Staging Areas for temporary buffering when direct movement is not possible.
  • Warehouse Process Types (WPTs) to control execution logic.
  • Door determination and staging processes to ensure the correct loading sequence.
  • Warehouse Monitor visibility to identify execution bottlenecks.

The objective is not simply to reduce storage time.

The objective is to execute warehouse movement without disrupting outbound commitments.

The Role of SAP TM

Cross Docking becomes significantly more effective when integrated with SAP Transportation Management (SAP TM).

SAP TM provides information such as:

  • Planned transportation routes
  • Vehicle arrival times (ETA)
  • Transportation Units
  • Freight Orders
  • Delivery priorities

This information allows warehouse execution to align with transportation planning before physical loading begins.

For example:

If SAP TM indicates that a vehicle will arrive two hours later than originally planned, immediately moving Cross Docking inventory to the outbound door may actually increase warehouse congestion.

Instead, SAP EWM can temporarily stage the material until transportation execution becomes synchronized again.

In this situation, synchronization creates greater operational value than immediate movement.

Temporary Staging Is Not a Failure

One misconception is that successful Cross Docking always means zero staging.

Operationally, this is not always true.

Temporary staging may be the correct business decision when:

  • Transportation schedules change.
  • Dock capacity becomes constrained.
  • Loading priorities change.
  • Labour availability decreases.
  • Multiple outbound shipments compete for the same resources.

SAP EWM provides flexibility through staging areas and storage types that allow warehouses to absorb operational variation while maintaining execution control.

Temporary staging is therefore not necessarily inefficient.

In many situations, it prevents warehouse disruption.

Visibility Drives Synchronization

Successful Cross Docking depends on continuous operational visibility.

Warehouse teams must understand:

  • Which inbound deliveries have arrived.
  • Which outbound deliveries require immediate execution.
  • Which Transportation Units are delayed.
  • Which staging locations are approaching capacity.
  • Which warehouse resources are available.

Without visibility, warehouse execution becomes reactive.

With visibility, warehouse execution becomes synchronized.

Business Value

When Cross Docking is viewed only as a speed optimization technique, warehouse teams may focus exclusively on reducing storage time.

When viewed as a synchronization strategy, different objectives emerge:

  • Reduce unnecessary handling.
  • Protect outbound service levels.
  • Improve dock utilization.
  • Align warehouse execution with transportation planning.
  • Maintain operational control during disruptions.

These outcomes provide greater long-term business value than simply moving goods faster.

Conclusion

Cross Docking should not be viewed simply as direct material movement.

Its real value lies in synchronizing warehouse execution with transportation execution while maintaining operational control.

SAP EWM provides the execution capabilities required to manage warehouse movement.

SAP TM contributes transportation planning and visibility.

Together, these systems enable coordinated execution rather than isolated movement.

In my view, the most successful Cross Docking operations are not those that move the fastest.

They are the ones that remain synchronized even when business conditions change.

Author: Rahul Kumar
Manyat Thinking

"Fast movement creates activity. Synchronized movement creates business value."
